title: Verb Types — RU-Verbs vs U-Verbs vs Irregulars

meta.focus: Identifying ichidan (RU-verb), godan (U-verb), and irregular verb classes to predict conjugation

sections.title: Verb Types — RU-Verbs vs U-Verbs vs Irregulars
sections.summary: Every Japanese verb belongs to one of three classes: ichidan (RU-verbs), godan (U-verbs), or irregular. Knowing a verb's class tells you exactly how to conjugate it into any form — ます, ました, ません, て-form, and all future forms.
sections.whyItMatters: You have already practised conjugating 行く, いる, and 来る in G5. But the same logic applies to every verb you will ever learn. Once you know the class of a verb, its conjugation is completely predictable. Getting this wrong means making systematic mistakes across every sentence you produce.
youWillLearn: Ichidan (RU-verbs): how to recognise them and form the stem by dropping る
Godan (U-verbs): how to recognise them and form the stem by shifting to the い-row
The る-trap: why some verbs ending in る are actually godan (the vowel test)
False ichidan exceptions: a handful of verbs that pass the vowel test but are still godan — these must be memorised
Irregular verbs: 来る, する, and ある — each must be memorised

sections.meaning: Ichidan (RU-verbs): drop る, then add the ending
sections.explanation: Ichidan verbs always end in る in dictionary form, AND the vowel sound immediately before る is always in the い-row (i) or え-row (e). The conjugation rule is the simplest possible: remove る, then attach the ending directly. The stem never changes.
notes: The dictionary form always ends in る, and the preceding vowel is い/え: い-ru (be/exist), き-ru (wear), み-ru (see), お-ki-ru (wake up), た-べ-ru (eat).
To form the ます stem: drop the final る. That's it.
いる → い (stem) → います / いました / いません / いませんでした.
The stem い is very short — just one sound — but it always works the same way.
Caution: not every verb ending in る is ichidan! See the る-trap in the comparison section.

examples.en: to be here → is here (polite present)
examples.breakdown: Ichidan: い (い-row before る) → drop る → い + ます = います
いる (ichidan): stem = い → います

sections.meaning: Godan (U-verbs): shift the final syllable to the い-row, then add the ending
sections.explanation: Godan verbs end in any う-row syllable in dictionary form: く, ぐ, む, ぬ, ぶ, う, つ, す — or る when the vowel before it is NOT in the い/え-row. To form the polite stem, shift that final syllable one column to the left in the hiragana chart (to the い-row): く→き, ぐ→ぎ, む→み, ぬ→に, ぶ→び, う→い, つ→ち, す→し, る→り. Then add ます.
notes: Full い-row shift map: く→き, ぐ→ぎ, む→み, ぬ→に, ぶ→び, う→い, つ→ち, す→し, る→り.
行く → 行き (く→き shift) → 行きます / 行きました / 行きません.
分かる → 分かり (る→り shift) → 分かります. Note: 分かる looks like ichidan but is godan! (See る-trap below.)
The stem changes every time you change the form — the final syllable always shifts.
All verbs NOT ending in る are definitely godan. Verbs ending in る may be either class.

examples.breakdown: Godan: く is in う-row → shift to き → 行き + ます = 行きます

examples.en: I go to the station every day.
examples.breakdown: 行く (godan): く→き → 行きます
分かる (godan る-verb): る→り → 分かります. Looks like ichidan but is NOT.

sections.title: The い-Row Shift — Godan ます Stem Map
sections.description: Godan verbs form the ます stem by shifting the final syllable from the う-row to the い-row on the hiragana chart. This is your first row shift — the same logic appears later for negative forms (あ-row) and other patterns. Master this table and every godan verb's polite form is instant.

rows.label: ぐ
cells: およぐ → およぎます

rows.label: す
cells: はなす → はなします

rows.label: る (godan)
cells: 分かる → 分かります

notes: Every ending shifts to the same consonant row, just with an い vowel. Think of the hiragana chart: く and き share the k-row; む and み share the m-row; す and し share the s-row.
The う ending shifts to い: 買う → 買います. No exception here — the い-row shift is perfectly regular for all 9 endings.
Ichidan (RU) verbs do NOT use this table. Their stem is formed by simply dropping る: 食べる → 食べ + ます.

This same row-shift logic will appear again in later grammar lessons for different rows (あ-row for negative forms, and others). Once you understand the hiragana chart pattern, each new shift is just a different target row.

pattern.label: IRREGULAR
pattern.text: 来る / する / ある

pattern.label: RESULT
pattern.text: → 来ます / します / あります

sections.meaning: Irregular verbs: memorise these — they do not follow standard ichidan or godan patterns
sections.explanation: Three verbs are irregular in Japanese N5. 来る (to come) is fully irregular: its reading changes completely in every conjugation (くる → きます). する (to do) is also fully irregular: it becomes します, not すります. ある (to exist — for inanimate things) looks regular in polite forms (あります follows the godan る→り pattern), but its plain negative is ない — not あらない — which is why it is classified as irregular.
notes: 来る (to come) is fully irregular. Its polite forms: 来ます (きます) / 来ました (きました) / 来ません (きません) / 来ませんでした (きませんでした). The kanji 来 stays but the reading changes completely to き.

する (to do) is fully irregular. Its polite forms: します / しました / しません / しませんでした. The stem し cannot be predicted from any rule — it must be memorised. する compounds (べんきょうする, etc.) follow the same pattern: べんきょうします.

ある (to exist, for things) conjugates regularly in polite forms: あります / ありました / ありません / ありませんでした — these all follow the standard godan る→り shift. Its irregularity appears in the plain negative: ない (not あらない, which the godan rule would predict). You will encounter this plain form in a later grammar lesson.
These three verbs appear constantly in Japanese — you will use them every day.
There is no other way to learn them except memorisation.

examples.en: to come → comes (polite present) — reading changes to き
examples.breakdown: Irregular 来る: cannot be predicted from the kanji reading くる → becomes きます

examples.en: to do → does (polite present) — stem し cannot be predicted
examples.breakdown: Irregular する: no godan/ichidan rule applies → し + ます = します

examples.en: There is a convenience store (over there).
examples.breakdown: ある: あります — polite forms follow godan る→り, but plain negative is irregular (ない)
Irregular 来る past: 来ました (reading: きました)

sections.title: Verb Type Comparison — Identifying and Conjugating
sections.description: Three verb types, one rule each. Use this table to identify any verb and predict its ます form.

rows.label: Dictionary form
cells: 来る / する / ある
rows.meaning: The form found in the dictionary

rows.label: Identifies as
cells: Ends in る; vowel before る is い (い-row) → ichidan
Ends in く (う-row syllable) → godan

rows.label: Stem formation
cells: 来る→き / する→し / ある→あり
rows.meaning: How to form the polite stem

rows.label: ます form
cells: 来ます (きます) / します / あります

notes: The る-trap: 分かる ends in る, but the vowel before る is か (あ-row, not い/え-row) → godan, not ichidan. Its stem is 分かり (る→り shift).
When a verb ends in る and you are unsure of its class, check the vowel before る: い/え-row = ichidan; any other row = godan.
ある's polite forms look regular (あり + ます follows godan る→り), but its plain negative is irregular: ない, not あらない. It is best memorised as a special case.

sections.title: The る-Trap: いる (Ichidan) vs 分かる (Godan)

items.label: いる — Ichidan (RU-verb)
points: Vowel before る: い — this is the い-row ✓
Polite form: います (not いります)

items.label: 分かる — Godan (U-verb, る-trap!)
points: Vowel before る: か — this is the あ-row, NOT い/え-row
Classification: GODAN despite ending in る
Stem: shift る→り → 分かり
Polite form: 分かります (NOT 分かます — that would be wrong!)

sections.tip: When you see a verb ending in る, first ask: 'What is the vowel before る?' If it is あ, う, or お → godan (shift る to り). If it is い or え → usually ichidan (drop る) — but beware: a small number of common verbs pass this test yet are still godan. See the next section for the exceptions you need to memorise.

sections.meaning: False ichidan — common verbs that pass the vowel test but are still godan
sections.explanation: The vowel test works for most verbs, but a small number of everyday verbs end in いる or える — exactly like ichidan verbs — yet conjugate as godan. These are called 'false ichidan' verbs. There is no rule to predict them; you must memorise the list. The good news: the list is short.
notes: きる (to cut) — godan: きります, きって. Compare きる (to wear) — ichidan: きます, きて. Same sound, different verb class!
はいる (to enter) — godan: はいります, はいって. The いる ending looks ichidan, but it is not.
かえる (to return home) — godan: かえります, かえって. The える ending looks ichidan, but it is not.
はしる (to run) — godan: はしります, はしって. Another しる ending that is godan.
しる (to know) — godan: しります, しって. Often used as しっています (knowing/state of knowing).
いる (to need) — godan: いります, いって. Compare いる (to exist) — ichidan: います, いて. Same sound, different class — just like the きる pair!
Strategy: when you learn a new verb ending in いる or える, always check its verb class in the dictionary. Most are ichidan, but these common exceptions are godan.

examples.en: to cut → cuts (godan: る→り shift, NOT る drop)
examples.breakdown: きる (to cut) looks ichidan but is godan: きり + ます = きります
きる (to wear) IS ichidan: き + ます = きます. Same dictionary sound as 'to cut', different class!

examples.en: to enter → enters (godan despite いる ending)
examples.breakdown: はいる: the いる ending looks ichidan, but this is godan → はいり + ます = はいります

sections.title: Identifying Verb Types in Natural Sentences

examples.context: Ichidan: いる (い before る)
examples.note: いる: い-row before る → ichidan → drop る → い + ます = います

examples.context: Godan: 行く (ends in く, not る — clearly godan)
examples.en: I go to the station today.
examples.note: 行く: ends in く (う-row) → godan → く→き → 行きます

examples.context: Godan る-trap: 分かる (か before る = あ-row → godan!)
examples.note: 分かる ends in る but か is あ-row → godan → る→り → 分かります

sections.title: かいわ 1: 毎日 どこ に 行きますか（Where Do You Go Every Day?）
sections.context: Yamakawa asks Rikizo about his daily routine. They discover they visit similar places.

lines.jp: りきぞさん は 毎日 どこ に 行きますか。
lines.en: Rikizo-san, where do you go every day?

lines.jp: いつも 駅 に 行きます。友だち が います よ。
lines.en: I always go to the station. My friend is there!

lines.jp: そうですか。先生 も 来ますか。
lines.en: I see. Does the teacher come too?

lines.jp: はい、時々 来ます。やまかわさん は どこ に 行きますか。
lines.en: Yes, sometimes. Where do you go, Yamakawa-san?

lines.jp: わたし は コンビニ に 行きます。
lines.en: I go to the convenience store.

lines.jp: 分かりました。いっしょに 行きます！

sections.title: かいわ 2: 週まつ に 何 を しますか（What Will You Do on the Weekend?）
sections.context: Rikizo and Yamakawa talk about their weekend plans. They mention friends visiting and places to go.

lines.jp: やまかわさん、週まつ に 何 を しますか。
lines.en: Yamakawa-san, what will you do on the weekend?

lines.jp: 友だち が 来ます。いっしょに ゲーム を します。
lines.en: My friend is coming. We will play games together.

lines.jp: いいですね！わたし は 店 に 行きます。
lines.en: That sounds nice! I am going to the store.

lines.jp: そうですか。駅 の 店 ですか。
lines.en: I see. The store at the station?

lines.jp: はい、そうです。スーパー も あります よ。
lines.en: Yes, that's right. There is a supermarket too.

lines.jp: 分かりました。わたし も 行きます！
lines.en: Got it. I will go too!

sections.title: Verb Type Challenge
sections.instructions: Identify verb types and choose the correct polite form.

items.q: 「いる」(to be/exist) — which verb class is this?
items.explanation: いる ends in る, and the vowel before る is い — the い-row. This confirms ichidan. Stem = い (drop る) → います / いました / いません.

items.q: 「分かる」(to understand) — which verb class is this?
items.explanation: 分かる ends in る, but the vowel before る is か — the あ-row. This is the る-trap! あ-row before る → godan. Stem = 分かり (る→り shift) → 分かります.

items.q: What is the correct polite present form of「行く」?
items.explanation: 行く is godan (ends in く). Godan stem rule: shift く to the い-row → き. Then add ます: 行き + ます = 行きます.

items.q: What is the correct polite present form of「来る」?
items.explanation: 来る is an irregular verb. Its polite forms cannot be predicted — the reading changes completely from くる to き. 来ます is read as きます. Memorise this form.

items.q: A new verb「おきる (おきる — to wake up)」ends in る. The vowel before る is き (き is in the い-row). What class is it?
choices: It looks like godan because of き
items.explanation: Apply the vowel test: the vowel before る is き — and き is in the い-row. い/え-row before る → ichidan. Stem = おき (drop る) → おきます.

items.q: Which statement about verb classes is TRUE?
choices: All verbs ending in る are ichidan (RU-verbs)
Verbs ending in る are ichidan ONLY IF the vowel before る is in the い/え-row
Godan verbs always end in く
Irregular verbs follow the godan stem rule with a special reading
items.answer: Verbs ending in る are ichidan ONLY IF the vowel before る is in the い/え-row
items.explanation: The る-trap: some verbs ending in る are actually godan. The vowel test: if the sound before る is in the い/え-row → usually ichidan. If it is any other row (あ, う, お) → godan. Example: 分かる (か = あ-row) is godan, NOT ichidan. Note: a few common exceptions exist — see the next question.

items.q: 「きる」can mean 'to cut' or 'to wear'. The verb 'to cut' is godan. What is its correct polite form?
items.explanation: きる (to cut) is a false ichidan — it ends in いる but conjugates as godan. Godan る→り shift: きり + ます = きります. Compare きる (to wear), which IS ichidan: き + ます = きます.

items.q: Which of these verbs is godan DESPITE ending in いる or える?
items.explanation: はいる (to enter) is a false ichidan — it ends in いる but is godan: はいります (not はいます). The others are all true ichidan verbs: たべます, みます, います. False ichidan verbs like はいる, かえる, はしる, and しる must be memorised.
